sanskrit_devanagari:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः ।
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः ॥ १२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
iṣṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ |
tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ || 12 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: In charge of the various necessities of life, the demigods, being satisfied by the performance of yajña [sacrifice], will supply all necessities to you. But he who enjoys such gifts without offering them to the demigods in return is certainly a thief.

In charge of the various necessities of life, the demigods, being satisfied by the performance of yajna [sacrifice], will supply all necessities to you. But he who enjoys such gifts without offering them to the demigods in return is certainly a thief.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3101 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The demigods are authorized supplying agents on behalf of the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Viṣṇu. Therefore, they must be satisfied by the performance of prescribed yajñas. In the Vedas, there are different kinds of yajñas prescribed for different kinds of demigods, but all are ultimately offered…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.12 ‘Yajna-bhavitah, being nourished, i.e. being satisfied, by sacrifices; devah, the gods; dasyante hi, will indeed give, will distribute; among vah, you; the istan, coveted; bhogan, enjoyments, such as wife, childeren and cattle.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-12-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: the gods give iṣṭān bhogān (desired enjoyments) when nourished by yajña; the one who consumes their gifts without offering back is verily a thief (stena eva saḥ). The cosmological-ethical structure makes consumption-without-contribution into theft. This is not metaphor; it is a structural claim about the wheel.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.12 ‘Pleased by the sacrifice,’ i.e., propitiated by the sacrifice, the gods, who have Me as their Self, will bestow on you the enjoyments you desire. Whatever objects are desired by persons keen on attaining release, the supreme end of human endeavour, all those will be granted by gods previously…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-12-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life (rain, sun, food, breath); offering back (yajña) is the proper return; not returning is misappropriation of what is His. stena eva saḥ — verily a thief. The verse's ethical-metaphysical structure makes consumption-without-offering into theft against the Supreme Person.

positions.rendering: thief; the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life — not returning offering is misappropriation of what is His

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree stena is the right rendering: thief. The verse uses sharp ethical-cosmological language. The disagreement is only in the metaphysical anchor of the obligation: the Advaita reading takes it as cosmological-ethical (the wheel's structure makes consumption-without-contribution theft); the Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as theft against the Supreme Person specifically (the devas are His agents). Engineering layers must preserve the sharpness — stena is not a soft word — without overclaiming the metaphysical anchor.

literal_meaning: Indeed, the gods, nourished by yajña, will give you desired enjoyments; one who enjoys what is given by them without offering back is verily a thief.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.12 sharpens the claim with the thief diagnosis. iṣṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ — the gods, nourished by yajña, will give you desired enjoyments. tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ — but one who enjoys what is given by them without offering back is verily a thief (stena). Per the Advaita reading: the verse fixes the obligation as cosmological-ethical; consumption without contribution = theft. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life; not returning offering is misappropriation. The verse uses sharp language: stena, thief.
